title: 2026 San Juan National Forest Prescribed Fire Program
field_incident_overview:
Spring Prescribed Fire Operations Are Complete.

The prescribed fire program will continue in Fall of 2026 as conditions allow.

The forests in Southwest Colorado are part of a fire-adapted ecosystem, which historically experienced frequent, low intensity fires on a large scale. Prescribed fire replicates that fire regime and increases the area on our landscape that has been burned at low and moderate conditions. These efforts reduce hazardous ground fuels, lessen the risk of unplanned large-scale wildfire, help restore ponderosa pine ecosystems, and improve wildlife habitat. Prescribed fire also reduces the ladder fuels that can carry fire into the canopy, killing mature trees. Prescribed fire smoke may affect your health. For more information, visit Wood smoke and your health.

Columbine Ranger District: 

The Columbine Ranger District will continue the following prescribed fire projects in Fall of 2026 as conditions allow. Smoke will be visible from Arboles, Bayfield, Pagosa Springs, as well as the U.S. Highway 160 and 151 corridors near those towns.

  • Sauls Creek: This project area includes multiple potential units totaling approximately 900 acres. This project is located in Sauls Creek near NFSR 608, east of Bayfield and south of Highway 160 in La Plata County. It is part of a larger fuels reduction strategy that has included commercial logging and other fuels reduction work. The Columbine Ranger District completed 255 acres on the Sauls Creek prescribed fire project March 28-29, 2026 and another 314 acres on April 24-25, 2026. 
  • Southern HDs Prescribed Fire: This project is approximately 2,000 acres in the Fosset Gulch area (NFSR 613), west of Chimney Rock National Monument in Archuleta County. Units will be burned using ground ignitions and possibly unmanned aircraft systems (drones) as well. In past years the Columbine District burned adjacent units to help reduce wildfire risk to local homes and infrastructure.

Pagosa Ranger District:

The Pagosa Ranger District will continue the following prescribed fire projects in Fall of 2026 as conditions allow.

  • Brockover-Devil Prescribed Fire: This 2,100-acre project area consists of "maintenance" burning, further reducing ground and ladder fuels. This treatment will build on the 2025 fall prescribed fire operations in the area and complement numerous mastication, thinning, and timber harvest treatments. These units are located along NFSR 629 and 923, west of Pagosa Springs and north of Highway 160. The Pagosa Ranger District completed 1,365 acres of this prescribed fire project April 23, 2026.
  • Echo Canyon Prescribed Fire: This 1,139-acre project is located along NFSR 029 southeast of Pagosa Springs and east of Highway 84. In 2018, this project area was the site of commercial thinning, followed by mastication work in 2021 and prescribed fire in 2023. This year, the District hopes to conduct a "maintenance" burn to maximize fuel reduction benefits. 
  • Eight Mile Mesa Prescribed Fire: This project is located on Eight Mile Mesa (NFSR 651), south of Pagosa Springs and west of Highway 84. 275 acres will be burned to clean up slash and forest litter following a 2022 oak mastication project as well as other hazardous fuels and timber harvest activities.

Dolores Ranger District: 

The Dolores Ranger District will continue the following prescribed fire projects in Fall of 2026 as conditions allow. During burning operations, smoke may be visible around Mancos, Dolores, Dove Creek, Cortez, and Highway 491 between Dove Creek and Pleasant View. Smoke may settle into low lying valleys and the Dolores River Canyon overnight.

  • Salter-Carlyle Prescribed Fire: This project is located 15 miles east of Cahone, near Salter Y along NFSR 510 in Montezuma and Dolores Counties. The Dolores Ranger District completed 1,660 acres on the Salter-Carlyle Prescribed Fire on May 20.
  • Boggy Draw Prescribed Fire: This 4,107-acre project is located 4-8 miles northeast of Dolores in Montezuma County, along NFSR 526 and 527. Smaller units of this overall project will be implemented as weather and conditions allow (see map). The Dolores Ranger District completed 1,586 acres on the Boggy Draw Prescribed Fire on April 219-20. The area closure was lifted on April 24.

title: Coconino National Forest Prescribed Burn Projects
field_incident_description:
Prescribed burns are important part of forest restoration and protecting communities by creating buffers around them. Prescribed burns and mechanical thinning work together to remove fuels such as woody debris and logging scraps from the forest floor during times of opportune weather.

Fire managers make every effort to effectively plan and execute burn plans at times when weather allows for smoke impacts to be minimized and transport up and over communities. If you have a complaint about the smoke impacts you are experiencing from any of the prescribed burn projects and would like to talk to a member of Fire Management Leadership, please call our hotline at 928-226-4608 and leave your message with your name and number and someone will return your call as soon as they are able.
field_incident_overview: No projects planned at this time.

title: Beaverhead-Deerlodge NF Prescribed Fire Operations
field_incident_description: Prescribed fire operations on the Beaverhead-Deerlodge National Forest.
field_incident_overview:
Every spring and fall, the Beaverhead-Deerlodge National Forest, along with its interagency partners and neighbors, plans to implement prescribed fire projects, as weather and fuels conditions allow. 


Prescribed fires are carefully planned and conducted in accordance with a written burn plan. Prior to implementation, Forest fire managers coordinate with local cooperators, county officials, and interagency partners. When prescription criteria are met, firefighters implement, monitor, and patrol each burn to ensure it meets forest health and public safety objectives. All prescribed fires will be implemented in compliance with Montana air quality standards and coordinated with the Montana Department of Environmental Quality and the appropriate county health departments to minimize the impacts of smoke. Smoke may pool in drainage bottoms and along highway corridors overnight; impacts are expected to dissipate in a few days.


Why use prescribed fires? Prescribed fires help reduce surface fuels, increasing landscape wildfire resilience and reducing the risk of uncharacteristic wildfire effects on the landscape. In addition, they improve and maintain forest health and wildlife habitat and eliminate invasive species. The forest ecosystems in the Northern Rockies have evolved with fire, with many of them relying on periodic fires to establish and maintain forest health. 

Benefits of prescribed fire as a forest management tool:
•    Protects communities and infrastructure by reducing hazardous fuels and the risk of future high-intensity wildfires.
•    Mitigates future wildfire risk and promote resilient fire-adapted landscapes.
•    Reduces build-up of flammable vegetation, dead and down trees, and overgrowth.
•    Improves and supports wildlife habitat for many species on the Forest.
•    Limits the spread of invasive plant species and minimizes the spread of pest insects and disease, maintaining native ecosystems.
•    Recycles nutrients back into the soil.
•    Promotes the growth of trees, plants, wildflowers, and other flora and fauna.
•    Continues the historic, natural fire regime of periodic disturbance by fire in forested ecosystems. 
 

How do we plan? Prescribed fires are planned for various units across the Forest; however, the window of opportunity for prescribed fire implementation is affected by several factors. Safety factors, weather conditions, air quality, personnel availability, and environmental regulations are continually monitored before implementation to determine feasibility of moving forward with the prescribed fire operations, during implementation, and after completion of the unit(s). 
 

Public notifications: Prior to initiating the prescribed fires, fire professionals assess conditions, conduct a test burn, and notify local governments and interested publics via website postings, email, social media, and news releases. Implementation and accomplishment updates will also be posted to the Forest website, Inciweb, and the Forest Facebook page.


Safety and monitoring: Trained fire professionals who have studied fire behavior and fire control techniques conduct prescribed fires in such a way to ensure the safety of the fire crew, nearby residents, and property.

title: Sheyenne National Grassland - Spring RX
field_incident_overview:
Prescribed Fires Planned at Sheyenne National Grassland

Sheyenne National Grassland is preparing for prescribed fire operations beginning later this month. These burns help reduce overgrown vegetation, lowering wildfire risk and protecting nearby communities, infrastructure, and natural resources.

What to Expect:

• Prescribed burns may occur April 27–May 15, as weather allows.

• 12 burn units, totaling 8,000 acres, are planned near McLeod and Hankinson, ND.

• No road closures are expected, but smoke may be visible from Highway 27 and surrounding areas.

• Portions of the North Country Trail will have short-term closures, with detours clearly signed for public safety.

• Some areas may remain closed for several days—watch for posted signs near burn sites.

Smoke will be visible during operations. For air quality updates, visit www.airnow.gov. If you encounter smoke while driving, please slow down and use your headlights.

All burns depend on the day‑of weather conditions and may be postponed if needed.

Stay updated through our website, social media channels, and InciWeb: https://inciweb.wildfire.gov/

County emergency managers will be notified when burning begins.

Learn more about Prescribed Burning on the DPG: 

https://www.fs.usda.gov/.../prescribed-burning-grasslands

title: Billy Fire
field_incident_overview:
This is the final update of the Type 3 team as they transfer command of the fire back to the local district as of tomorrow morning. 64 personnel remain assigned to the Billy Fire.

Unless there are significant changes in fire activity, this will be the last daily update of the Billy Fire.

-

The Billy Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire on the Tonto National Forest, 15 miles south of Young, Arizona. Firefighters are taking a full suppression strategy on the fire situated on the east side of the Sierra Ancha Mountains.

title: Moon Complex
field_incident_description: Wildland Fire
field_incident_overview:
Evacuations: Curry County Sheriff John Ward has removed all evacation levels for the Moon Complex. 

The Emergency Area and Trail closure for the Moon Complex that went into effect November 14th of 2025, has been rescinded. Effective, February 24th, 2026, this closure was replaced with Emergency Trail Closure 06-10-28-26-04, which re-opens many of the camping areas along the lower Rogue River Corridor. While much of the fire-impacted area has been reopened, recreationists will continue to see effects from the fire and ongoing repair work in the area. 

For closure information, visit: https://www.fs.usda.gov/r06/rogue-siskiyou/alerts/rogue-river-national-recreation-trail-emergency-closure 

The remaining Rogue River Trail Closure begins at the western trailhead of the Rogue River Trail (Trail #1160) and follows the trail to the east for approximately 8 miles to Jackson Creek. The trail from Jackson Creek to Clay Hill Creek will be opened by May 1, 2026. The Forest will be assessing the segment from Clay Hill Creek to Foster Bar in the spring of 2026. 

On September 3, a lightning event on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest caused a number of fires in the vicinity of the Rogue River about 11 miles northeast of Agness, Oregon. Six of these made up the initial Moon Complex: the Backbone, Brushy, Pinnacle, Tate, Paradise, and Stair fires. 

The Moon Complex is now in 100% containment status. 

Fire Restrictions: Tuesday, October 16, 2025, The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest decreased the Fire Danger Rating to LOW across all five Ranger Districts and removed Public Use Restrictions forest-wide as well as on the Wild & Scenic section of the Rogue River. To learn more, visit the forest website: https://www.fs.usda.gov/alerts/rogue-siskiyou/alerts-notices

Fire Repair: Repair of fire line and roadways is the primary focus of resources engaged on the Moon Complex

title: Rabbit Mountain Prescribed Burn
field_incident_description:
The Rabbit Mountain prescribed burn is a maintenance-focused project designed to uphold and enhance the fuels reduction work previously completed in the area. By reintroducing low‑intensity fire under controlled conditions, land managers can continue to reduce hazardous surface fuels, limit encroaching vegetation, and maintain the desired forest structure established through earlier treatments.
This maintenance burn is especially important given the presence of critical energy infrastructure and nearby communities in the Durango area. Sustaining reduced fuel loads helps lower the risk of high‑severity wildfire that could threaten these assets. The project also supports the long-term health of valuable natural resources, including ongoing Ponderosa pine regeneration that relies on regular, low-intensity fire to thrive.
The Rabbit Mountain RX ensures that past work continues to provide meaningful protection and ecological benefits, keeping the landscape resilient and better prepared for future fire on terms that support both community safety and forest health.
field_incident_overview:
Update: 4/6/2026

United States Wildland Fire crews are planning prescribed burn operations in the Rabbit Mountain area over the coming days, pending favorable weather and site conditions.

Unit 2 will be assessed for potential ignition during this operational period. This prescribed fire will help reduce hazardous fuels, improve ecosystem health, and support long‑term wildfire risk reduction.

Smoke may be visible from nearby communities and roads during ignitions and as interior fuels continue to burn. Fire personnel will be on site throughout the operation to ensure conditions remain within prescription.

Updates will be shared as conditions and timing are finalized.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Update: 3/18/2026

 

Rabbit Mountain prescribed fire operations have been completed for the day, with 207 acres successfully treated. Crews will remain on scene to patrol and monitor the burn area to ensure all fire lines continue to hold and conditions remain secure.

Thank you for your continued awareness as prescribed fire operations support community safety and landscape health.

--

Fire crews successfully held and monitored yesterday’s burn operations, patrolling the fire’s edge and eliminating any remaining heat sources. All fire lines held overnight, and firefighters will continue patrolling and monitoring those areas today.

With favorable weather, ignitions are underway in Unit 3. This increased activity means smoke will be more noticeable today. Please use caution when traveling near smoky areas and visit AirNow.gov for current air quality information.

Why prescribed burning matters at Rabbit Mountain:
Prescribed fire reduces hazardous fuels, lowering the risk of catastrophic wildfire. It also improves habitat conditions and supports long‑term ecosystem resilience. In this area, prescribed burning is especially important because of nearby communities and critical energy infrastructure that require added protection from high‑severity wildfires.

Thank you to all our partners for the support to make this prescribed burn happen, and that thank you for the continued public support.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 

Update: 3/17/2026

Ignitions for today are now complete. Fire crews successfully treated 79 acres under good weather conditions, and operations went smoothly.

Smoke will remain visible in the area and may settle into low‑lying areas overnight. Please use caution when traveling nearby, especially after dark. Crews will continue patrolling today’s burn area until late this evening.

Fire personnel will return tomorrow to conduct ignitions on the next unit.

Thank you for your continued support, and please check back for updates.

-

U.S. Wildland Service Firefighters and partners are conducting prescribed burn operations on Rabbit Mountain today. Weather conditions are favorable, and this morning’s test fire was successful, so ignitions are now underway.

Smoke will be visible in the area throughout the day. Please use caution when traveling nearby, as smoke may settle into low‑lying areas overnight and reduce visibility.

These operations help maintain forest health and reduce future wildfire intensity.

Thank you for your support!

-----------------------------------------------------------

Update: 3/16/2026

Rabbit Mountain Prescribed Burn – Ignitions Begin Tomorrow (Units 1 & 3, 286 acres)

U.S. Wildland Fire Service firefighters plan to begin ignitions tomorrow on Rabbit Mountain Units 1 and 3. Recent good weather and fuel conditions have created a safe window to move forward with this 286‑acre maintenance burn.

This operation supports long‑term community protection by reducing hazardous fuels, maintaining previously treated areas, and helping safeguard infrastructure and surrounding communities

Light to moderate smoke may be visible in the area. Please use caution when traveling nearby and be aware of changing visibility conditions.

This is a planned, carefully managed burn designed to strengthen forest health and reduce the risk of high‑intensity wildfires later in the season.

Stay tuned for updates on the Rabbit Mountain project.

-------------------------------------------------------------

Update: 3/5/2026

Seasonal weather patterns across La Plata County are shifting toward conditions favorable for implementing the Rabbit Mountain prescribed burn. Cooler nighttime temperatures, more consistent daytime warming, and predictable humidity trends are improving fuel receptivity within the project area. The Rabbit Mountain RX consists of four burn units totaling approximately 586 acres, which may be treated individually or in phases depending on daily conditions. As weather and fuel conditions align, firefighters will take advantage of these operational windows to safely conduct broadcast burning within the established prescription parameters.

Prescribed fire is an essential tool for maintaining the health and resilience of fire‑adapted ecosystems in the Durango region. Good fire helps reduce hazardous fuel buildup, improve forest structure, and restore natural fire processes that have been absent from the landscape. By applying fire in a controlled, deliberate manner, land managers are able to reduce the risk of high‑intensity wildfires and improve the ability to manage future fire on our terms rather than during peak wildfire season.

Smoke impacts from prescribed fire are typically limited in duration and intensity compared to long‑lasting smoke events generated by uncontrolled wildfires. Completing prescribed burns under the right conditions helps protect nearby communities by reducing the potential for large, destructive wildfires that produce heavy smoke for extended periods. Prescribed fire is also far more cost‑effective to implement than the significant expenses associated with wildfire suppression, emergency response, and landscape rehabilitation.

Stay tuned and informed for future notifications about upcoming burning planned for the Rabbit Mountain project.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

RABBIT MOUNTIAN PRESCRIBED BURN UPDATE 9/29/2025

🔥 Prescribed Burn Alert – Rabbit Mountain, CO 🔥

The BLM Tres Rios Field Office is planning a prescribed burn of 586 acres at Rabbit Mountain, northwest of Bayfield, Colorado. If conditions allow, operations may begin as early as mid to late this week.

This burn is part of our ongoing efforts to: 

✅ Reduce wildfire risk 

✅ Improve wildlife habitat 

✅ Promote healthy vegetation

Crews will be on-site during ignition and will monitor the area for several days. Smoke will be visible during daylight hours and may settle in low-lying areas overnight.

All operations depend on favorable weather and fuel conditions. Thank you for your awareness and support as we work to protect and restore our public lands.

Please check back for continued progress updates of the Rabbit Mountain prescribed burn!

title: 2026  Huron-Manistee NFs Spring/Summer Prescribed Fire Season
field_incident_overview:
The Huron-Manistee National Forests Fire and Aviation Management Unit is preparing for prescribed fire operations.

Rx burns are planned events that are matched to predetermined weather/fuel parameters. We will evaluate weather conditions in the hours before a burn begins. If conditions warrant, scheduled prescribed fire activities may be canceled. Updates including cancellations, additions and corrections will be added to this post.

Prescribed fires reduce risk to the public by removing hazardous vegetation to mitigate wildfires and prevent loss of property. They also help to re-establish fire’s positive role in local ecosystems, increase native plant regeneration and improve wildlife habitat and forest health. 

During active burning, smoke and flames may be visible from roads and in areas downwind of the burn site. Residents may experience smoke during the prescribed burns. title: Road 203 Fire
field_incident_overview:
Road 203 Fire has been declared controlled. 

The Road 203 Fire was detected at 4:00 pm Thursday, March 12, 2026 on the Bessey Ranger District of the Nebraska National Forest. Ground crews initially responded and received assistance from air resources conducting retardant and water drops. Rocky Mountain Complex IMT 3 assumed command of the fire on Saturday, March 14, 2026.

The Road 203 Fire is burning in heavy dead and down timber and grass. The terrain is steep and remote, making it challenging for firefighters to get in or out of the incident. A Nebraska National Forest closure order is in effect.

title: Morrill Fire
field_incident_overview:
This page will no longer be updated after 6 p.m. on Saturday, March 28, 2026. Please contact the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency at 402-326-3179 with questions regarding the Morrill Fire. 

During the late afternoon of Thursday, March 12, 2026, a wildfire started in Morrill County, Nebraska. The fire was rapidly pushed to the east/southeast by extremely high winds. Despite the heroic efforts of firefighters and community members, the fire extended into Garden, Arthur and Keith Counties. In less than 12 hours, the fire traveled approximately 70 miles. Winds shifted to the northeast early Friday morning, March 13 and the fire is close to the western edge of Grant County. Air tankers arrived on scene early Friday morning. Local law enforcement agencies issued evacuation orders in their jurisdictions and evacuation shelters were established. Agricultural irrigation systems were activated in some areas to try to slow the fire’s spread and protect resources and structures. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires March 14. Rocky Mountain CIMT 1 managed the Ashby, Minor, Cottonwood and Morrill Fires under the direction of the State of Nebraska. The State Emergency Operations Center at the Nebraska Emergency Management Agency remains fully operational to coordinate with Rocky Mountain CIMT 1.

On Saturday, March 28 at 6 p.m. local time, Rocky Mountain CIMT 1 will transfer management responsibility of all four fires in a formal process called transfer of command. Command of the Morrill Fire will be transferred to local fire departments. Command of the Ashby Fire, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and the initial-attack response will be transferred to Rocky Mountain CIMT 2.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

You can find information on resources available to farmers and ranchers impacted by the Nebraska wildfires by clicking on the following blue link Help. Additional information will be added when available. If you’d like to suggest an addition of a resource, please email agr.webmaster@nebraska.gov.

title: Cottonwood Fire
field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started in the southeast corner of Lincoln County during late afternoon on Thursday, March 12 and entered Dawson County later that night. Winds from the west were very high and quickly spread the fire.  Numerous evacuations took place as the fire rapidly spread to the south/southeast. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. Firefighters and community members heroically fought the fire since it started.  

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) assumed management of the Morrill and Cottonwood Fires on March 14.  At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires. Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

Resources will remain available to provide statewide initial attack response. As with its predecessor, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 will manage all fires under the direction of the State of Nebraska.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

title: Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest Prescribed Fire Operations
field_incident_description: Prescribed fire operations on the Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest
field_incident_overview:
2026 Prescribed Burn Operations 

The Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest, along with interagency partners and neighbors, plans to implement prescribed fire projects, as weather and fuels conditions allow. 

Public and firefighter safety is the top priority in all prescribed fire operations. Fire managers develop burn plans accounting for safety, specific fuel and weather prescriptions, and smoke management. Prior to implementation, local fire managers coordinate with local cooperators, counties, and partners.  All prescribed burns are closely evaluated and are only approved when favorable conditions are present.

Benefits of prescribed fire as a forest management tool:

  • Protects communities and infrastructure by reducing hazardous fuels and the risk of future high-intensity wildfires.
  • Mitigates future wildfire risk and promote resilient fire-adapted landscapes.
  • Reduces build-up of flammable vegetation, dead and down trees, and overgrowth.
  • Improves and supports wildlife habitat for many species on the Forest.
  • Limits the spread of invasive plant species and minimizes the spread of pest insects and disease, maintaining native ecosystems.
  • Recycles nutrients back into the soil.
  • Promotes the growth of trees, plants, wildflowers, and other flora and fauna.
  • Continues the historic, natural fire regime of periodic disturbance by fire in forested ecosystems. 

Prescribed fires are planned for various units across the Forest; however, the window of opportunity for prescribed fire implementation is affected by several factors. Safety factors, weather conditions, air quality, personnel availability, and environmental regulations are continually monitored before implementation to determine feasibility of moving forward with the prescribed fire operations, during implementation, and after completion of the unit(s). 

All prescribed fires will be implemented in compliance with Oregon air quality standards and coordinated with appropriate county health departments to reduce the impacts of smoke to neighbors, cooperators, and surrounding communities. Smoke may settle in valley bottoms and drainages overnight, but it is expected to dissipate within a few days. Visit the Fire and Smoke Map by AirNow for the current smoke outlook. Daily planned ignitions can be found on line at the PNW Prescribed Fire Tracker. 

Types of Prescribed Fire

Pile Burning: ignition of hand or machine piles of cut vegetation that have been allowed to dry out. Pile burning is commonly used to mitigate wildfire risk in areas where broadcast burning may not be feasible, such as adjacent to communities or private lands. Piles are made from the residual woody debris often left after a mechanical thinning treatment and can range in size from small hand stacked piles to large piles created with mechanical equipment. Piles are typically burned in fall, winter, or early spring after the piles have dried out and conditions allow, reducing the damage to the remaining trees and confining the majority of fire to the footprint of the pile.

Under-burning: a type of prescribed fire treatment, that ignites vegetation under the forest canopy and focuses on the consumption of surface fuels.

title: Ashby Fire
field_incident_overview:
A large wildfire started after midnight on Thursday, March 26 approximately 2 miles north of Ashby in Grant County. Due to high winds, it quickly ran 17 miles southward and burned into Morrill County and the area already burned by the Morrill Fire. An evacuation of Ashby took place as structures were threatened. Road closures occurred due to the impact of fire and smoke. 

The Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 1 (CIMT1) mobilized initial-attack resources from the nearby Morrill and Cottonwood Fires. They actively engaged the fire and assisted local volunteer fire departments and the Nebraska State Fire Marshal’s Wildland Incident Response and Assistance Team. At Nebraska Governor Jim Pillen’s request, Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) 1 assisted with both the Ashby and nearby Minor Fire. 

The incident has additional initial-attack groups staged and ready to immediately respond to requests from local or state officials to assist with the initial suppression of any other new fires that may start. 

 At 6 p.m. MDT March 28, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 assumed command of the Ashby, Minor and Cottonwood Fires and provided statewide initial attack response.  Under the direction of the State of Nebraska, Rocky Mountain CIMT 2 transferred management responsibility of the Ashby, Minor, and Cottonwood Fires to local volunteer fire departments on Sunday, April 5, at 6 a.m. local time. Local jurisdictions will also be responsible for the initial and ongoing suppression of any new fires that may start.

On Friday, March 13, Governor Jim Pillen is issued a statewide burn ban, to help reduce the potential for additional wildfires in Nebraska. Dry and windy conditions have caused the spread of significant fires in the central and western parts of the state. On March 26, the Governor’s executive order was extended through April 10, 2026.

Please avoid the fire area and do not interfere with firefighters while they are working. When travelling, please watch for firefighters and their vehicles as they operate along roadsides.

title: Sago Fire
field_incident_overview: The Sago Fire is a full suppression incident. It is currently burning in grass as well as ponderosa pine, piñon, and juniper trees. Crews are actively working to improve and keep the fire within established retardant lines, handlines, and dozer lines. Repair work began on Tuesday, March 31, 2026. Command of the Sago Fire on the Mescalero Reservation was transferred to the Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Incident Management Team Friday at 5 p.m.

title: 2026 Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison National Forests Prescribed Fire Program
field_incident_overview:
Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison (GMUG) National Forests are planning prescribed fire operations this spring, pending all required approvals. We estimate this work to continue through the summer as weather conditions allow. We use prescribed fires to help reduce overgrown vegetation to help protect local communities, infrastructure and natural resources from wildfires, while also supporting healthier, more resilient communities. 

The listed fire projects are not presented in chronological order. title: Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest 2026 Prescribed Fires
field_incident_description: 2026 prescribed fire operations on the Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest.
field_incident_overview:
The Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, along with their interagency partners and neighbors, plans to implement prescribed fire projects, as weather and fuels conditions allow. 

Public and firefighter safety is the top priority in all prescribed fire operations. Fire managers develop burn plans accounting for safety, specific fuel and weather prescriptions, and smoke management. Prior to implementation, local fire managers coordinate with local cooperators, counties, and partners.  All prescribed burns are closely evaluated and are only approved when favorable conditions are present.

Benefits of prescribed fire as a forest management tool:

  • Protects communities and infrastructure by reducing hazardous fuels and the risk of future high-intensity wildfires.
  • Mitigates future wildfire risk and promote resilient fire-adapted landscapes.
  • Reduces build-up of flammable vegetation, dead and down trees, and overgrowth.
  • Improves and supports wildlife habitat for many species on the Forest.
  • Limits the spread of invasive plant species and minimizes the spread of pest insects and disease, maintaining native ecosystems.
  • Recycles nutrients back into the soil.
  • Promotes the growth of trees, plants, wildflowers, and other flora and fauna.
  • Continues the historic, natural fire regime of periodic disturbance by fire in forested ecosystems. 

Prescribed fires are planned for various units across the Forest; however, the window of opportunity for prescribed fire implementation is affected by several factors. Safety factors, weather conditions, air quality, personnel availability, and environmental regulations are continually monitored before implementation to determine feasibility of moving forward with the prescribed fire operations, during implementation, and after completion of the unit(s).

title: Southwest Kansas Wildfire Outbreak
field_incident_description: Wildfire across three counties in Southwest Kansas. The counties of Morton, Meade, and Clark.
field_incident_overview:
On May 14, 2026 a powerful storm system moved through southwest Kansas. Lightning strikes, fueled by strong winds and very dry vegetation, started multiple fires across the landscape. The fires quickly surpassed the local and mutual aid response. State assistance was requested. Aviation, KFS DFMOs, and in-state Wildfire Taskforces were mobilized. Many of the initial fires merged and are now being managed as a complex fire. Kansas Governor Kelly issued a State of Emergency Declaration on Friday, May 15, 2026. Local declarations have been issued; there is no federal emergency declaration. 

On Monday, May 18, the Meade County Fires are now under one Incident Command System and will be called the Meade Lake Complex. 

On May 20, 2026, the fire region received precipitation and higher humidity, which helped increase containment on both complexes. Non-local resources will be returned to their home units, and the fires will be monitored and patrolled by the local fire departments. The weather conditions in southwest Kansas have not dramatically changed. There is still potential for new starts to burn intensely and grow rapidly. 

The fires under the Meade Lake Complex are the Wolf Canyon Fire, the Meade County Complex 1 and 2, the Proffitt Lake fire, and the 2 unstaffed fires in the southern part of the county.  

Meade Lake Complex -  Jeff Latham and Troy Mueller - Incident Commanders      

  • 91,616 acres
  • 100% contained

The 500-Acre Pond Fire and the Herman Range Fire grew together on Sunday May, 17, 2026. All fires in Clark County will also be a complex of fires. The complex will include the Bouziden Range Fire and the Lexington Fire

Herman Range Complex - Chad Maybery Incident Commander

On Monday, May 18, 2026, the Herman Ranch Fire made a significant run to the south when the forecasted cold front impacted the fire’s open containment lines along the southern edge of the incident. The acres and map do not reflect that event.

title: 2026 BAER Seven Cabins
field_incident_description: Burned Area Emergency Response
field_incident_overview:
The Seven Cabins Fire started on May 14, 2026, in the Capitan Mountain on the Smokey Bear Ranger District on the Lincoln National Forest. Initial attack resources from the Lincoln National Forest, Smokey Bear Ranger District responded. Shortly after the initial attack, Southwest Area Complex Incident Management Team 1 (SWCIMT1) was ordered due to the fire burning in rugged inaccessible terrain of the Wilderness area and assumed command on Friday, May 15th. The management of the fire transitioned to Southwest Area Complex Incident Management Team 2 (SWCIMT2) on Saturday, May 30th at 6am.

The fire is being suppressed using direct extinguishment and indirect confinement strategies. Firefighters are continuing containment efforts across the fire including mop-up operations. Crews will continue to expand defensible space around structures and values at risk while also improving holding lines.

In the last few days, suppression resources have made significant progress constructing and reinforcing containment lines. More favorable weather conditions have resulted in lower fire intensity in many areas. Suppression repair in select areas of the fire is in progress and will increase in the coming days. 

On Sunday May 31st, the U.S. Forest Service Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER) Team arrived to identify and manage potential risks to resources on National Forest System lands and reduce these threats through appropriate emergency measures to protect human life and safety, property, and critical natural or cultural resources.

title: Poorman Prescribed (Rx) Fire
field_incident_overview:
Update: Poorman Prescribed Fire (June 24 at 4:00 p.m.)

Cooler temperatures overnight allowed for good relative humidity recoveries, which moderated fire behavior this morning. Temperatures rose throughout the day causing some smoke to be visible coming from the interior of the unit but there are no concerns for fire spread or breach of containment lines. Smoke impacts to the surrounding communities remain minimal.

Today crews patrolled the perimeter of the unit and scouted for additional heat sources to expose and secure in preparation for the incoming precipitation.

For questions about the Poorman Prescribed Fire, call 208-861-3465

Background
The Poorman Prescribed Fire is a 2,692‑acre fuels‑reduction project designed to lower the risk of high‑intensity wildfire near the communities of Crouch and Garden Valley. This area has been successfully treated with prescribed fire in the past, including the late 1990s and again from 2010–2012.

This project is one of several active forest management efforts on the Boise National Forest to reduce wildfire risk and support firefighter safety. In spring 2026, seven prescribed burns were successfully implemented across the Boise National Forest.

Ignitions for the Poorman Prescribed Fire took place over four days from late April through early May. Firefighters have worked daily to patrol the control line to reinforce it against any nearby fire activity that could possibly threaten it. The fire has been meeting treatment objectives, consuming pine needles, downed logs, brush, and some grasses. However, unusually dry weather in May has led to smoke lingering in the Garden Valley area longer than anticipated.

Current Situation
Due to ongoing dry conditions and continued smoke impacts, firefighters are taking additional steps to reduce fuels and shorten the duration of smoke in the area:

• Perimeter work: Beginning June 2, firefighters widened the existing handline around the project boundary by carefully burning grasses along the edge. This work was completed on June 3 and further reduces the likelihood of fire moving outside the project area.

• Interior burning: Firefighters will be burning remaining grasses inside the project area starting June 4, with work expected to continue for up to four days. This may temporarily increase smoke but will help reduce future smoke production and speed up the overall clean‑up period.

Going Forward

This InciWeb site will be updated regularly while the prescribed fire is being patrolled. For questions about the Poorman Prescribed Fire, call 208-861-3465

title: Santa Rosa Island Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER)
field_incident_description:
On Friday, May 15, 2026, an aircraft flying over Santa Rosa Island in Channel Islands National Park reported a wildfire. National Park Service (NPS) staff already on the island confirmed it later that morning. The Santa Rosa Island Fire was located on the southeastern end of the island in remote and rugged terrain. This was a full-suppression human-caused wildfire and remains under investigation. The fire was fully contained on June 4.

A team of Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER) specialists were on the island through June 5-10, evaluating post-fire conditions and identifying immediate risks. The team includes engineers, ecologists, hydrologists, archaeologists, and wildland fire specialists who will assess soil stability, watershed conditions, infrastructure damage, threats to natural and cultural resources, and other post-fire impacts. Their findings will help guide emergency stabilization efforts and inform decisions about public access.
field_incident_overview:
Current Conditions:
On 6/16, the Burned Area Emergency Response (BAER) team presented their recommendations and funding specifications to Channel Islands National Park staff. These emergency stabilization recommendations will will help park staff prioritize actions needed to reduce risks including erosion, flooding, hazardous trees, and impacts to sensitive resources. Using information collected during their evaluations, the team identified immediate threats to life, safety, property, and critical natural and cultural resources that may be caused by the fire. While the park will implement time-sensitive projects over the summer, many projects will last for years, as the island recovers from the fire.
 

Closures:
Santa Rosa Island is closed to all day and overnight use through at least June 30, 2026, due to the wildfire. All Water Canyon Campground reservations have been cancelled through this date and visitors have been notified by email. Campground reservation holders with bookings through August 14, 2026, have also been informed that the wildfire may affect their stay. If additional cancellations are needed, they will receive follow-up emails.

Backcountry camping within the southeastern quadrant of Santa Rosa Island will remain closed for the remainder of the 2026 recreation season.

title: Lansing Wildfire
field_incident_overview:
The Lansing Wildfire was reported in the early morning of Thursday, June 4, 2026. The fire is currently being managed by the Forest Service, with support from the Pennsylvania DCNR, several McKean County Volunteer Fire Departments, the Crawford Erie County Wildland Fire Crew, and the PA DCNR D15 Wildland Fire Support Crew.

The wildfire is located in McKean County, within the Bradford Ranger District of the Allegheny National Forest. The entirety of the fire is burning on federal Forest Service lands, located near Forest Road 268, just east of Pennsylvania Route 321. 

[June 5, 2026 at 3:00 PM] The Lansing Wildfire is estimated at 38 acres and is 40% contained. Smoke may be visible from the area. 

[June 8, 2026 at 10:00 AM] The Lansing Wildfire remains at 38 acres and is 80% contained. The Allegheny National Forest fire staff, along with wildland fire resources from Maine and West Virginia, are continuing patrol and mop-up activities today, as weather permits. Precipitation is forecasted Wednesday, providing beneficial moisture for firefighting operations. No structures have been impacted by the fire. Smoke may still be visible, as areas are still smoldering. 

[June 9, 2026 at 10:00 AM] The Lansing Wildfire is now 90% contained. The fire has transitioned from Type 4 to a Type 5 fire, due to the reduced complexity. Resources on scene today are Allegheny National Forest fire staff, West Virginia State Type 6 engine, and Wildland Fire Service Type 6 engine from Acadia National Park, Maine. Fire personnel continue to mop-up as needed. Forecasted rain the next couple of days should assist with dry conditions. 

[June 10, 2026 at 2:00 PM] The Lansing Wildfire is now 100% contained. The area received 0.2 inches of rain in the last 24 hours, providing beneficial moisture to fuels. The Allegheny National Forest fire staff continue to monitor and mop up the effected area.

title: South Fork Fire
field_incident_overview:
The South Fork Fire ignited on June 9, 2026, in the Soldier Creek Wilderness area of the Nebraska National Forest’s Pine Ridge Ranger District near Crawford, Nebraska. Local responders, U.S. Forest Service crews and volunteer firefighting partners engaged the fire early as dry fuels, steep terrain and strong winds drove rapid spread. U.S. Highway 20 was closed by the Nebraska Department of Transportation as the fire jumped the highway. A red flag warning was in effect through Wednesday, June 10.

By June 10, the fire had grown to nearly 9,000 acres and Nebraska’s Governor Pillen officially issued an emergency declaration for the South Fork Fire. It was fueled by rugged terrain, dry vegetation including timber and grassy undergrowth, and strong winds. On June 11 at 6 am the Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 2, under the command of Jay Mickey, assumed control of the fire.

On June 12, containment increased to 7% even as the fire grew to about 28,067 acres. Resources including National Guard units, ground crews, dozers and aircraft focused on defending the towns of Crawford and the Fort Robinson State Park area. The Red Cross established community shelter options at the Chadron State College–Andrews Hall.

By June 15, containment was at 55% and the fire had grown to 38,914 acres. 581 personnel were supporting the fire. Forecasters predicted that the week ahead would bring sustained winds anticipated between 23 and 30 miles per hour, and the possibility of gusts escalating to as high as 50 miles per hour. 

Nebraska’s Governor, Jim Pillen received an operational visit of the South Fork Fire on June 16. He visited ICP and was provided with a comprehensive briefing. He had the opportunity to communicate with members of his constituency as well as incident team members. 

Containment reached 80% on June 17, with the acres climbing to 39,057. Although conditions continued to change, fire activity was generally quiet. A NIROPS flight (a thermal imaging aircraft used to map active wildfires) was ordered after several areas of hot spots were discovered. 

Line is now in place around the entire fire perimeter. Crews are working to secure and mop up the containment line and will continue for several days. Suppression repair work is also u begin underway and will continue in the next few days. 

On June 20th the overall acreage of the South Fork Fire increased to 39,696 due to more accurate satellite mapping showing previously unseen grassy areas and containment increased to 90%.

Rocky Mountain CIMT2 transfered command to the Nebraska Type 3 team on Sunday, June 21, 2026, at 6 p.m.  

Please follow the Nebraska National Forest on Facebook and the Nebraska National Forest website's Alerts and Notices page for further updates.

title: Bear Fire
field_incident_overview: On June 9, 2026, aerial and ground firefighting resources began engaging in suppression actions on the Bear Fire, a wildfire located east of Mangus Mountain on the Quemado Ranger District of the Gila National Forest. The Gila Las Cruces Type 3 Incident Management Team, led by Incident Commander, Marcus Cornwell, assumed command of the Bear Fire on June 11, 2026.

title: Snake River
field_incident_description:
The Snake River Fire began on the evening of June 12th, 2026. The fire worked its way along Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston, in light flashy fuels and steep rocky terrain. Hand crews continued to gain access to the fire to cold trail, a method of controlling a partly extinguished fire edge by carefully inspecting and feeling with the hand for heat to detect any fire, digging out every live spot, and trenching any live edge, the perimeter of the fire throughout the day to increase containment. Crews worked on gridding 150 feet interior of the fireline around the entire perimeter of the fire extinguishing any heat they found. With the rugged terrain and unseasonably hot temperatures, crews had to navigate at a slower pace in order to safely work the fireline. Throughout the day state mobilization crews also assessed the structures threatened in the Moses community. With minimal fire activity throughout the day, two 20-person hand crews were reassigned to other nearby evolving incidents.

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.

Evacuations and Closures: No evacuations are in effect at this time and there are no road closures

Weather: Temperature: High 91/Low 55. Mainly clear skies. Winds NE at 10 to 15 mph.
field_incident_overview:
The Snake River Fire was reported at 5:55 PM on June 12th, along the Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston.  The fire quickly progressed north in flashy fuels (grasses and sagebrush) and in steep, rocky terrain, threatening rangeland, powerlines, communication infrastructure, rail infrastructure, and residences as Moses community.  Crews from Whitman County Fire Districts 14 and 12, Asotin County, DNR, and the nearby cities of Lewiston, Clarkson and Asotin engaged the fire, with the aid of a type 1 and type 2 helicopters.  State mobilization was authorized at 9:00PM on June 12th, coordinating additional district crews to respond to the fire. 

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.

title: Rock Canyon Fire
field_incident_description:
The Rock Canyon Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire that was discovered on June 15, 2026.
Fire originated approximately 9 miles south of Highway 89 and 9 miles west of House Rock Valley Road in Coconino County, AZ
The fire's predominant movement has been eastward pushed by dry gusty winds from the west/northwest, largely parallel with the Winter Road.

Personnel are on scene from the U.S. Wildland Fire Service (USWFS), operating in coordination with the Bureau of Land Management, the Kaibab National Forest (USFS), and the Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management and local fire agencies are working to suppress this wildfire.

Firefighter and public safety remain the highest priority.
field_incident_overview:
Rock Canyon Fire was discovered on June 15, 2026, at 1:47 PM. 

The Color Country Type 3 Incident Management Team (IMT) will be transitioning to a local Type 4 incident management organization June 25, 2026. 

Fire Area Closures for both the Bureau of Land Management - Arizona Strip District and the U.S. Forest Service - Kaibab National Forest will be lifted effective 0600 on June 25, 2026. 

There will be no further updates on the incident here or on social media unless unforeseen activity warrants.

title: Dellenbaugh Fire
field_incident_overview: The Dellenbaugh Fire began the afternoon of Monday, June 15. The fire is burning in the Grand Canyon–Parashant National Monument south of Mount Dellenbaugh.

title: Upriver Fire
field_incident_overview: The Upriver fire started at 12:17pm on Tuesday June 16, 2026. The fire is located approximately 1 mile east of Spokane, Washington. Local resources responded from multiple agencies. Northeast Washington Interagency Team 2, under Incident Commander Nolan Roth, took command of the fire at 8 am on Wednesday June 17.

field_incident_description:
This is a lightning caused fire with a full suppression strategy.
The fire is burning in primarily locust, with heavy dead and down fuels.
field_incident_overview:
The Rio Fire is a lightning caused fire located on the Espanola Ranger District of the Santa Fe National Forest in Mesa De La Gallina three miles northeast of Chicoma Mountain. It was reported on June 16, 2026. 

The Northern New Mexico Type 3 Incident Command Team (IMT) took command of the Rio Fire on Friday June 19 at 0600.

title: Grapevine
field_incident_overview: The Grapevine Fire is burning in timber in the Clover Mountain Wilderness Area, 10 miles Southeast of Caliente, Nevada.

title: Kane Springs
field_incident_overview: The Kane Springs Fire is burning in short grass and brush, 17 Miles Southwest of Caliente, NV.

title: Sycamore
field_incident_overview: The Sycamore Fire was discovered Monday, June 15, 2026, north of Globe on the Globe Ranger District. Firefighters are utilizing both direct extinguishment and indirect confinement methods as part of a full-suppression strategy. Ground resources are being utilized where the probability of success is highest due to the remote location, steep rugged terrain and poor access.

title: Kartar Fire
field_incident_description: Wildfire
field_incident_overview: Kartar Fire - South and East of Omak Lake on the Colville Reservation, approximately 15 miles SE of Omak, WA.

title: Pocket Fire
field_incident_description: The Pocket Fire, located approximately 7 miles north of Sedona, Arizona was report at 501 pm on Friday, June 19, 2026.  The wildfire grew rapidly in brush, juniper and ponderosa pine, prompting the Coconino National Forest ordering the Northern Arizona Type 3 Incident Management Team.  Soon after, the fire made a run uphill towards Oak Creek Canyon and the recommendation to the Coconino County Sheriffs Department to order a "Go" evacuation status for Oak Creek Canyon.  This additional complexity prompted the ordering of the Southwest Complex Incident Management Team 2 to manage the fire.
field_incident_overview:
Official Fire information can be found here on InciWeb at https://inciweb.wildfire.gov/incident-information/azcof-pocket-fire or on the Coconino National Forest Facebook page at https://www.facebook.com/CoconinoNF 

The Pocket Fire is located 7 miles north of Sedona.

The latest evacuation information can be obtained from Coconino County at coconino.az.gov/ready and the latest road closure information can be obtained from the Arizona Department of Transportation at az511.com.

title: Iron
field_incident_description:
The Iron Fire is burning near Eureka in Juab County, Utah. The fire has been active under hot, dry conditions and has affected the Eureka area. Firefighters are working to protect life and property, support the Eureka community, conduct structure protection where needed, and construct and improve containment lines around the fire.
Current suppression actions include direct and indirect line construction, line preparation, and aviation support as conditions allow. Hot and dry weather continues to contribute to active fire activity.
field_incident_overview:
The Iron Fire is burning near Eureka in Juab County, Utah. 
Great Basin Team 3 assumed command of the incident at 0600 on June 22, 2026. Firefighters are working to protect life and property, support the Eureka community, and construct and strengthen containment lines as conditions allow.

title: Garred Road Fire
field_incident_description: The fire started north of Highway 2 near Coulee City. Crews worked all night to secure line around the fire. The winds pushed the fire south across Highway 2 despite heavy air resources working to control the area. The fire continued to progress south across Highway 17 into the Dry Falls and Sun Lakes area, which caused Level 3 evacuations and closure of the state park, which have since been reduced to Level 1 evacuations and opening of the park.
field_incident_overview:
The Garred Road Fire is burning in Grant and Douglas counties near Coulee City, Washington. 

SE Washington IMT3, team 2 assumed command of the incident at 6:00 am on June 23, 2026. The fire started Sunday, June 21 and was bolstered by winds on Monday, June 20.

title: Kinky Creek Fire
field_incident_overview:
Teton Interagency Fire crews responded yesterday afternoon to suppress the Kinky Creek Fire, located approximately three miles west of Darwin Ranch, near upper Gros Ventre Falls in the Gros Ventre Wilderness on the Jackson Ranger District of the Bridger-Teton National Forest. The fire is burning in timber on a steep slope, and yesterday exhibited active fire behavior with torching, spotting, and running, quickly growing to an estimated 350-acres. 

Yesterday, smokejumpers were able to create an anchor point at the heel of the fire and are working to keep the fire on the west side of the Gros Ventre River. An anchor point is a secure location that firefighters establish to begin building fireline from. Other resources began setting up structure protection around Darwin Ranch.

Firefighting resources include a type 3 helicopter, air attack, smokejumpers, fire engines, and other support staff, with over 50 firefighters working to suppress the fire. Additional resources have also been ordered and will arrive today. 

Fire managers are implementing direct extinguishment and indirect confinement strategies on the Kinky Creek Fire. A direct extinguishment strategy is when firefighters engage the active flame front directly to stop fire spread. An indirect confinement strategy is when firefighters work away from the active flame front to steer or contain the fire.

A type 3 incident management team has been ordered to help with firefighting efforts and should arrive by this evening. A type 3 incident management team is a multi-agency organization of firefighting specialists that manage incidents of moderate complexity that require more resources and a longer operational duration than the local forest can handle alone.

The public is asked to avoid Kinky Creek Road; Kinky Creek and Clear Creek trails; and Brewster, Lunch, and Chateau lakes so fire crews can work safely and efficiently. A forest closure order of the area is forthcoming.

The fire danger rating is currently “high” for the Teton Interagency Dispatch area, which includes Bridger-Teton National Forest, Grand Teton National Park, and the National Elk Refuge.

For other news, events and information, visit the Bridger-Teton National Forest website and follow the Bridger-Teton on Facebook and X @BridgerTetonNF.

Smoke may be visible in Teton, Sublette, and Fremont counties. Please do not report.

title: McCauley Springs Fire
field_incident_overview:
The McCauley Springs Fire was detected Wednesday morning June 24, 2026, on the Jemez Ranger District. The fire is exhibiting high spread potential and is very active with fire characteristics ranging from creeping to running, to torching and spotting. 

Evacuations/Closures: Coordinating with Sandoval County, evacuations are in place. Evacuations will be posted on the Sandoval County website.

This is a rapidly evolving incident, and more information will be released as it becomes available.

title: Cottonwood Fire
field_incident_overview: The Cottonwood Fire started on June 22, 2026 and experienced rapid growth fueled by incredibly dry fuels and weather conditions favorable to fire spread. First responders worked to evacuate the fire area on June 23. Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 5 assumed command of the Cottonwood Fire on June 24, 2026. Weather conditions and dry fuels will continue to be a major factor in the coming days as crews work to create containment.
